observations.recommendation:
Given the circumstances affecting the fair presentation of PPE accounts in the
financial statement of the Municipality, we recommended and Management
agreed that:

a. Develop and institutionalize an Asset Management Manual with clear
policies on property acquisition, documentation, and reporting; defined roles
for key personnel, and standardized procedures for verifying, recording, and
reconciling land accounts with supporting documents.

b. For the PPE items, conduct a physical count of PPE to identify the existence
of those items. Then, reconcile the result thereof with the recorded items on
the financial statements. Finally, draw the proper journal entry to reflect the
actual book value and accumulated depreciation.

c. For the parcels of land, ensure the Proper Registration and Documentation
of Government-Owned Land.











































































































































Ensure that:








Secure certificates of title for the four (4) recorded parcels of land
amounting to ₱35,187,500.00 to establish indisputable ownership.
Verify whether the five (5) titled parcels of land totaling 86,185 square
meters are included in the ₱11,977,997.84 lump-sum amount or remain
unrecorded. If unrecorded, determine the specific land areas composing
the lump sum.
Conduct a reconciliation between the Land Ledger Card, Statement of
Financial Position, and land title records to identify and correct
discrepancies.
Initiate processing of certificates of title for unregistered parcels of land,
with the responsible committee providing status updates and
recommending necessary actions.

d. Prevent risks related to ownership disputes and project delays by expediting
land titling to avoid legal disputes and unauthorized occupation and ensure
that land titles are secured before government-funded infrastructure projects
begin to prevent delays.

e. Create a committee/team that will account for all titled land on which the
MGL may exercise ownership rights, acquired through law, sale, donation,
transfer, and other forms of conveyance.

f. For the CIP Accounts, address historical items (2017-2024) as follows:








Form a Technical Working Group to review long-standing CIP accounts
and recommend necessary adjustments.
Develop a phased approach to clearing backlog CIP items, prioritizing
those with the most significant financial impact.
Draw the proper journal entry vouchers, based on the above
reconciliations, to reclassify the CIP accounts to their corresponding PPE
and depreciation accounts. This is to reflect the correct book value
thereof.

To address the identified gaps, we recommended and Management agreed to
provide the following instructions to the accounting personnel:

a. Reclassify the Current Portion of the Loan to Non-current. The following
journal entry is recommended:
Debit: Current Loans Payable-Domestic ₱1,942,568.28
Credit: Non-Current Loans Payable-Domestic ₱1,942,568.28

b. Update the Notes to the Financial Statements to include detailed information
on the loan’s key terms such as the breakdown of principal and interest, the
quarterly amortization schedule, and collateral details as well as risk
management practices related to this liability. This should be done in
accordance with relevant IPSAS standards, particularly IPSAS 30: Financial
Instruments: Disclosures.

c. Establish a quality control mechanism by implementing a pre-submission
review. Given the current single staffing of the Accounting Office, an interim
measure could involve a review by a designated senior official in another
relevant department (e.g., the Treasurer or Budget Officer) with sufficient
financial understanding. Utilize a standardized checklist aligned with IPSAS 1
and IPSAS 30 to ensure all key loan disclosure items are accurately reported.

d. Revisit the Plantilla of the Accounting Office and prioritize the hiring of
additional accountant positions to facilitate the establishment of effective and
segregated review mechanisms.

To address the noted deficiency, we recommended and management agreed to:

a. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to check the veracity of entries and trace
the cause of the unreconciled recurring variances and prepare adjusting
entries to correct the Due from/ Due to Other Funds account; and

b. Install control mechanism to be embedded within the reporting process to
ensure that balances of reciprocal accounts are reconciled and any
discrepancy detected shall be immediately resolved or adjusted to maintain
accuracy and reliability of account balances.

To address the deficiencies and mitigate the associated risks, we recommended
and Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Strengthen the internal control system by documenting and implementing
specific control activities related to cash management, in accordance with the
New Government Accounting System (NGAS) Manual, including roles and
responsibilities of accountable officers, specific procedures/processes,
timelines, control mechanisms and sanctions for non-compliance.

b. Direct all collecting officers to remit collections in full and prepare the RCD
at the close of each business day to the LO to ensure complete and accurate
recording and reporting of transactions by the Accounting Office. Moreover,
direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit intact all collections to AGDB daily
or the next banking day.

c. Ensure that reconciliations of cash balances are conducted between the
accountable officer and the accountant at least quarterly, or more frequently if
required by management.

To address these observations and mitigate associated risks, we recommended
and Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Develop, Implement, and Strictly Enforce Comprehensive Internal Control
Policies for Cash Advance Management.

b. Enforce strict liquidation policies immediately by requiring all accountable
officers to settle the outstanding cash advances totaling ₱33,928,510.81 within
a defined timeframe of 60 days. Simultaneously, implement an immediate
suspension of new cash advances for any officer or employee with overdue
balances until full liquidation is achieved. Communicate these deadlines and
the consequences of non-compliance through official memoranda and
meetings.

c. Intensify efforts to recover advances from separated personnel by sending
formal demand letters (via registered mail), exploring engagement with
relevant government agencies (e.g., GSIS), and pursuing legal action when
necessary. Strengthen exit clearance procedures to ensure all financial
accountabilities are settled before clearance is granted, holding accountable
those who approve clearance with unresolved advances. Exhaust all recovery
efforts before requesting COA write-offs, with thorough documentatio

d. Conduct regular and mandatory training on PD 1445 and COA Circulars
related to cash advances. Hold accountability seminars emphasizing the legal
and fiscal repercussions of non-compliance for both Accountable Officers and
their supervisors.

e. Task the Municipal Accounting Office with monthly reconciliation and
generation of detailed aging reports (by officer and overdue duration).
Submit these reports to the Agency Head and relevant department heads for
review and action. Enhance transparency by considering quarterly public
disclosure of overall liquidation status (aggregated data).

f. Implement a tiered system of sanctions (e.g., salary holds, formal
reprimands) for delayed liquidations, ensuring due process. Integrate
compliance with cash advance policies into performance evaluations and
promotion considerations.

To address the noted deficiencies and identified gaps, we recommended and
Management agreed to take the following actions:

a. Enforce compliance to the accounting guidelines in accordance with the
NGAS Manual and strengthen the Inventory Management System by
documenting specific control activities, including roles and responsibilities,
specific procedures/processes, timelines, control mechanisms, and sanctions
for non-compliance.

b. Consider upgrading the current accounting software to include the feature on
SLC maintenance and weighted average computation of inventory cost,
otherwise manual system shall be used for the maintenance of SLC and
computation of inventory cost.

c. Reassess manpower complement in the MGSO and match it with the
existing operational and regulatory demands and workloads.

d. Include in Inventory Management Manual, the processes, necessary
documentations and control measures related to the receipt of inventories
from other agencies (i.e. DOH/ PHO) and eventual consumption or transfer
thereof to other agencies (LGU/ NGOs/POs).

We recommended and Management agreed to the following: 1. Requiring advance approval on the face of the cash advance voucher that said advance” in the face of the DVs. 2. Issuing demand letter/s for cash advances already due and demandable and withhold salary releases if the official/employee still fails to liquidate the cash advance within the given time frame; and 3. Consider appointing a Special Disbursing Officer/s, other than the Municipal Treasurer and those functions, for programs and activities.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed, with the Offices of the Mayor, Municipal
Accountant and Municipal Treasurer as lead, enforce and implement the rules and
regulations pertaining to the grant, liquidation and/or settlement of cash advances to
exact accountability by:

a. requiring approval on the face of the cash advance voucher that said
official/employee has no previous unliquidated cash advance;
b. issuing demand letter/s for advances already due and demandable and
withhold salary releases if the official/employee still fails to liquidate the cash
advance within the specified time frame; and
c. consider appointing a Special Disbursing Officer/s, other than the Municipal
Treasurer and those functions, for programs and activities.

We recommended and Management agreed, with the Offices of the Mayor and
Municipal Accountant as lead, ensure that cash advances of those who are and would-
be separated from the service are settled by enforcing measures provided/allowed by
existing regulations, such as issuing demand letters, requiring clearances and
withholding of salaries, to impose compliance.

Hence, we recommended and management agreed to:

a. Require the Office of the Municipal Treasurer to render the monthly reports such as
Disbursement Vouchers (DVs) with supporting documents, Payrolls, Official Receipts
and other liquidation documents including revenue reports and supporting
documents to the Office of the Municipal Accountant on or before the 5th day of the
ensuing month to allow its prompt recording in the books of accounts; and
b. Instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to
ensure prompt submission of DVs and other related reports, undertake recording in
the books of accounts, and submit the same within 10 days of the ensuing month to
the Office of the Auditor.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Disaster Risk
Reduction Management Officer, the Municipal Planning and Development Officer
and other concerned officials, to develop a progressive, comprehensive and
sustainable local disaster PPAs and ensure that the planned PPAs for disaster risk
reduction are necessary, highly doable, attainable and fully funded during the target
implementation period to alleviate the Municipality’s preparedness for natural
calamities, man-made hazards and disasters. Henceforth, maximize the utilization of
the accumulated unexpended balance of DRRMF that will provide optimum benefit
to the constituents, and achieve a disaster resilient community.

and the MAO, in view of the gaps observed and raised in audit, to consider the:

a. conduct of periodic Physical Count of PPE items by directing concerned
offices to perform the comprehensive inventory taking of items under their
responsibility, and the preparation of the RPCPPE by the Inventory

unserviceable PPEs to serve as bases in dropping the PPE items in the books
of accounts and to process the gain/loss computation on the sale of the
subject property; and
h. drawing of necessary adjusting journal entries to adjust the income
recognized arising from the disposal of PPE items that eventually caused
the overstatement of the Government Equity balance.

1. Revisit the guidelines relative to the utilization of the LDRRMF to ensure and
enforce the strict compliance thereof;
2. Review and assess the projects, programs and activities with respect to their
compliance with existing laws, rules and regulations as a requirement before
these undertakings are endeavored to form part of the LDRRMFIP; and
3. Identify projects, programs and activities, with a view of the funding
constraints, by assessing which to prioritize and implement vis-à-vis the LGU
determined disaster related issues.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Strengthen the controls being adopted pertaining to the proper identification
and recording of transactions by diligently reviewing the nature and purpose
of each transaction in order to reflect their true and correct balances; and
2. Draw the necessary adjusting journal entries, in view of the preceding
discussions and/or presentations in order to facilitate the proper classification
of transactions to their proper accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Exert more efforts in identifying the cause of the unreconciled balance in order
to correct the reported amount and be able to draw the necessary adjustments
the soonest possible time; and
2. Review the existing controls on cash management and identify weak points to
improve on, by adopting more stringent measures including but not limited to
timely and regularly keeping tab of book and bank balances that have remained
unreconciled, and so to afford the reconciliation thereof in due time.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
1. Look into the matter by considering the possibility of prioritizing the use of
the fund to projects, programs or activities that are most relevant in
addressing the disaster-related issues frequenting the locality; and
2. Make representation with other related agencies or organizations and confer
with them for possibilities on making partnership arrangements in the
implementation of necessary projects, programs or activities aimed at better

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed that in view of the operating realities of the LGU to:
a. Identify the key priority programs of the Municipality in terms of socioeconomic development and environmental management through needs assessment in line with its vision, goals and objectives and taking into consideration the limited resources available;
b. Evaluate the previous developmental projects and determine how these projects affect in the development of the Municipality, and be able to provide opportunity for the enhancement of the proposed development projects; and
c. Revisit DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 for guidance in the identification of priority development projects.
d. Carefully plan out the drawing of infrastructure development projects to lessen the annual cost of repairs and maintenance arising from dilapidated and/or worn-out and still in-used structure;
e. Do away with small and frequent expenditures and instead have the amount lumped and allotted for concrete and tangible projects; and
f. Review its utilization of the said fund vis-à-vis the prevailing law, rules and regulations.
g. Fast track the implementation of development projects, and make necessary evaluation as to why some have not been implemented to date.
h. In view of the operating realities, to improve on the aspect of project monitoring and evaluation, whenever warranted, by involving the stakeholders and/or the people where the projects are situated. By doing so, apart from empowering them, collection of information could be made easier.
i. The initiative of the MPDO and MEO, and in view of the operating realities to re-evaluate the status of the LGU’s projects, activities and programs. Ascertain the constraints that were seen to have deterred the supposed timely implementation thereof. Formally document in the Risk Registry, if any, the identified impediments and/or limitations and have them timely updated as ready reference for future undertakings.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended and Management agreed to improve the processes of planning, prioritization and budgeting of SEF through:
Instituting measures to make the LSB more proactive and effective in introducing educational reforms within its capacity;
The formulation of development programs on education for the Municipal Government, articulating the collective vision of the major stakeholders;
Increasing participation and involvement of various sectors, including but not limited to parents, teachers, non-teaching personnel, and among others, in the regular consultative meetings of the LSB;and
Strictly requiring the submission of the respective schools’ budget proposals together with their annual appropriations from the national government to be able to properly identify the items for funding augmentation;

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Develop and implement measures to make the processes of disbursements more transparent by adhering as much as possible to the law, rules and regulations; and
Refrain from accommodating expenditures which do not fall within the recommended programs, activities and projects chargeable against the SEF.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Draw necessary adjusting journal entries to reclassify the previously recorded transactions in order to reflect the same in their correct account classifications; and
Enhance the LGU’s accounting control on the classification of accounts in order to prevent the recurrence of misclassifying accounts.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Enhance accountability of the acquired and issued assets and/or properties by conducting regular physical inventory of the same, and evaluating on a timely basis the status and issuance of these subject assets to monitor their whereabouts and serviceability; and
Revisit the relevant guidelines on SEF and related rules and regulations on property, supply and inventory management for the proper disposition and issuance of LGU procured assets;

We recommended and Management agreed to hasten the development of measures, within its capacity, and through the LSB and the Municipal Planning Development Office (MPDO), to assess projects, activities and programs with respect not only to the desired outputs, but as well to the outcomes and impacts these projects have to the locality.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Fast track the revision of the SWMP based on the newly adopted standard and submit the same to NSWMC for review;
Regularly practice the earlier drafting, preparation and submission of the SWMP, with a view of the previously obtained feedbacks and/or comments from the concerned approving authority.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Adopt measures for the immediate closure of the open dump in compliance with the Law, Rules and Regulations;
Draft policies and/or guidelines on the wearing of protective gears and equipment for personnel during collection and disposal of wastes; and
Strengthen the measures to address the problem on burning of solid wastes in the dump site. Also, conduct information drive/campaign regarding the proper management of solid wastes; the effects and/or impacts it bring to the quality of life.

We recommended and Management agreed that in view of the operating realities of the LGU to:
Identify the key priority programs of the Municipality in terms of socio-economic development and environmental management through needs assessment in line with its vision, goals and objectives and taking into consideration the limited resources available;
Evaluate the previous developmental projects and determine how these projects affect in the development of the Municipality, and be able to provide opportunity for the enhancement of the proposed development projects; and
Revisit DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 for guidance in the identification of priority development projects.

We recommended and Management agreed that, in view of the operating realities of the LGU to:
Carefully plan-out the drawing of infrastructure development projects to lessen the annual cost of repairs and maintenance arising from dilapidated and/or worn-out and still in-used structures;
Do away with small and frequent expenditures and instead have the amount lumped and allotted for concrete and tangible projects; and
Review its utilization of the said fund vis-à-vis the prevailing law, rules and regulations.

We recommended and Management agreed to fast track the implementation of development projects, and make necessary evaluation as to why some have not been implemented to date. Moving forward, with respect to the current budget year, we are highlighting the need for the Management, which they likewise agreed to make necessary reviews and assessment of the items included as development projects in the LGU’s AIP for the current year, and effect the necessary amendment should it require any.

We recommended and Management also is in agreement that, in view of the operating realities, to improve on the aspect of project monitoring and evaluation, whenever warranted, by involving the stakeholders and/or the people where the projects are situated. By doing so, apart from empowering them, collection of information could be made easier.

We recommended and Management agreed that through the initiative of the MPDO and MEO, and in view of the operating realities to re-evaluate the status of the LGU’s projects, activities and programs. Ascertain the constraints that were seen to have deterred the supposed timely implementation thereof. Formally document in the Risk Registry, if any, the identified impediments and/or limitations and have them timely updated as ready reference for future undertakings.

We recommended and Management agreed to:
Immediately stop the processing/granting of loans to all Municipal employees considering that the Terms and Conditions of the MOA were strictly followed.
Effect the necessary deductions to those employees who have existing loan balances. While for those who are no longer in service, take measures in collecting the receivables from them or their co-maker, if warranted.
Lastly, adopt measures that could give long term solution to the employees’ financial problems such as, but not limited to, conducting orientation on financial management and livelihood trainings.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the Municipal Mayor ensure the timely submission by the Municipal Accountant of all disbursement vouchers for cash advance for Confidential and Intelligence Fund with its complete supporting documents to the concerned COA ATL in a sealed envelope within seven (7) working days from release of check as specified in Sections 6.1.8.4 and 6.3.3d of the Joint Circular 2015-01.
observations.managementAction: Submission within the reglementary period in a sealed envelope with the necessary label required.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management require the officials concerned to abide with basic requirements under laws, rules and regulations on supporting documents for claims of creditors to vouch for their propriety and validation pursuant to Section 4 (6) of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction: Submitted the lacking Supporting Documents.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management require the Treasury, Accounting and other concerned offices to strictly adhere with the prescribed time frame for the submission of accounts and financial reports to the Auditor concerned pursuant to Section 6.0 of COA Circular No. 95-006; COA Circular No. 96-011; Section 346 of RA 7160 and other relevant provision in the NGAS Manual to enable us to make a timely decision in audit.
observations.reason: Lack of manpower the Accounting Section to finish reports time, however, with all the efforts exerted, the number of months delayed reduced to less than one (1) month.
